articles.title: Lehi Wakes Again
articles.author: Dorothy J. Roberts
articles.complete_text:
Lehi wakes again in these deep eyes,
For dawn unraveling his shroud of night
Reveals arroyos where his red dust lies,
His fables crumbling in the mesa's light.

He walked in his decreed immunity
Spared, when his fair kin met oblivion's waves;
Through sinful eras, saved by chastity,
He built his hogan on the fair ones' graves.

Penanced with clouded sight and darkened skin,
He roamed, lost courier of the distant dead;
Stalking the centuries in moccasin
He left few symbols save the arrowhead.

Lehi looks again toward the promised land
From persecution's wide and bitter sea.
Calm is the spirit tried on trackless sand
Where patience hardens into majesty.

His fair, white kinship to its source will trace
Through blood remembered, when his mind is freed.
Lehi lives in this long-chastened race
To fill the covenant upon his seed.
articles.notes: This is a complete poem about Lehi, formatted as a frontispiece poem with clear stanzas and poetic structure

articles.title: The Fluorescence of the Lamanites
articles.author: Elder Spencer W. Kimball
articles.complete_text:
Of the Council of the Twelve
[Address Delivered at the Annual General Relief Society Conference, October 1, 1952]

of Israel, and thank the Lord for you, and for the glorious work that you are doing in Zion. I come to realize more and more, as my experience broadens, the vast influence and power that a small minority may wield in this world, in politics, in religion, in social activities, everywhere you go. A small group, united in purpose, with definite goals, may greatly influence the great majorities.

Sister Spafford mentioned that the subject of my talk would be the Indian Relations work. I should like to broaden that slightly, and discuss with you the minority program.

Last year the brethren saw fit to reorganize the proselyting program within the stakes of Zion. The First Presidency and the Council of the Twelve were brought into the program with the First Council of the Seventy, and some additional prestige has come to this great program. The 35,000-odd conversions in the past fifteen years under the First Council of the Seventy have proved the power of this work.

I feel that we have hardly scratched the surface in the proselyting endeavor in the stakes of Zion. Now that stakes are scattered from ocean to ocean, from the north to the south, and we have strong leadership and many people, there is no reason now why we cannot reach out and bring into our numbers the millions of good, honorable people of the world, who are searching for the truth.

The stake missionary program, as now reorganized, is under the direction of President Stephen L Richards of the First Presidency. President Joseph Fielding Smith is the chairman of all of the missionary endeavor. There is a sub-committee on the stake missionary work, and included in the stake missionary work is the proselyting of the minority groups.

This is not limited to the Indians, though in many of the stakes, perhaps half of the stakes of Zion, the Indian program looms the largest of all the minority groups. However, we have far more Lamanite people speaking the Spanish language in the stakes of Zion, and there are areas where the Japanese are predominant, and there are other minority groupsâ€”the Greeks, the Italians, and others. It is the purpose of the stake missionary work to reach out and proselyte, and bring into the Church these many wonderful people.
articles.notes: This appears to be the beginning of a conference address by Elder Kimball about missionary work among minority groups, particularly Lamanites. The text cuts off mid-sentence but represents substantial article content.

misc_summary: The remaining content includes: reader letters in 'From Near and Far' section, Relief Society General Board listing with officers and staff, table of contents for February 1953 issue, and publication/subscription information including editorial offices and mailing details.

This file contains story continuations from other articles ('A Time to Forget' and 'Trite, But True'), advertisements for music, piano sales, Hawaii tours, book clubs, baby formula, and a contest announcement for designing nursing uniforms at BYU. All content appears to be legitimate miscellaneous material - no standalone articles to extract.
